Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

## ORTHOGONALISING GRADIENTS TO SPEEDUP NEURAL NETWORK OPTIMISATION

## **Anonymous authors**

Paper under double-blind review

## ABSTRACT

The optimisation of neural networks can be sped up by orthogonalising the gradients before the optimisation step, ensuring the diversification of the learned representations. We orthogonalise the gradients of the layer’s components/filters with respect to each other to separate out the intermediate representations. Our method of orthogonalisation allows the weights to be used more flexibly, in contrast to restricting the weights to an orthogonalised sub-space. We tested this method on ImageNet and CIFAR-10 resulting in a large decrease in learning time, and also obtain a speed-up on the semi-supervised learning BarlowTwins. We obtain similar accuracy to SGD without fine-tuning and better accuracy for na¨ıvely chosen hyper-parameters.

## 1 INTRODUCTION

Neural network layers are made up of several identical, but differently parametrised, components, _e.g._ filters in a convolutional layer, or heads in a multi-headed attention layer. Layers consist of several components so that they can provide a diverse set of intermediary representations to the next layer, however, there is no constraint or bias, other than the implicit bias from the cost function, to learning different parametrisations. We introduce this diversification bias in the form of orthogonalised gradients and find a resultant speed-up in learning and sometimes improved performance, see fig. 1.

Figure 1: An example of the speed-up obtained by orthogonalising the gradients on CIFAR-10.

Our novel contributions include this new optimisation method, thorough testing on CIFAR10 and ImageNet, additional testing on a semi-supervised learning method, and experiments to support our hypothesis.

In section 2 we detail the method and results to give an understanding of how this method works and its capabilities. Then, in section 3, we provide experimental justifications and supporting experiments for this method along with finer details of the implementation and limitations.

## 2 OVERVIEW OF NEW METHOD AND RESULTS

## 2.1 RELATED WORKS

Gradient orthogonalisation has been explored in the domain of multi-task learning (Yu et al., 2020) to keep the different tasks separate and relevant. However in this work we focus on orthogonalisation for improving single task performance.

Weight orthogonalisation has been extensively explored with both empirical (Bansal et al., 2018; Jia et al., 2017) and theoretical (Jia et al., 2019) justifications. However, modifying the weights during

1

training is unstable, and, in addition, it limits the weights to a tiny subspace. Deep learning is know to work well despite the immense size of the weight space, and as such we do not view this as an advantage. Xie et al. (2017) obtain improved performance over Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) via weight orthogonalisation and allows them to train very deep networks, we aim to achieve the same thing while being more flexible with model and optimisation method choice. We do this by orthogonalising the gradients before they are used by an optimisation method rather than modifying the weights themselves.

2.3 RESULTS

## 2.3.1 CIFAR-10

We trained a suite of models on the CIFAR-10 (Krizhevsky et al., 2009) data set with a mini-batch size of 1024, learning rate of 10 _[−]_[2] , momentum of 0.9, and a weight decay of 5 _×_ 10 _[−]_[4] for 100 epochs. We then repeated this using Orthogonal-SGDM instead of SGDM and plot the results in figs. 2 and 3 and table 1.

Orthogonal-SGDM is more efficient and achieves better test accuracy than SGDM for every model we trained on CIFAR-10 without hyper-parameter tuning. The validation curves follow the training curves, figs. 4 and 5, and have the same patterns, this means that Orthogonal-SGDM exhibits the same generalisation performance as SGDM. learns much faster at the beginning of training, as shown by fig. 2, this means that we do not need as many epochs to get to a well-performing network. This is especially good in light of the large data sets that new models are being trained on, where they are trained for only a few epochs, or even less (Brown et al., 2020).

For SGDM the performance of the residual networks designed for ImageNet (Deng et al., 2009) (18, 34, 50) get worse as the models get bigger. The original ResNet authors, He et al. (2015), note that unnecessarily large networks may over-fit on a small data set such as CIFAR-10. However, when trained with Orthogonal-SGDM, these models do not suffer from this over-parametrisation problem and even slightly improve in performance as the models get bigger, in clear contrast to SGDM. This agnosticism to over-parametrisation helps alleviate the need for the practitioner to tune a model’s architecture to the task at hand to achieve a reasonable performance.

## 2.3.2 MATCHING RESNET’S PERFORMANCE

Having shown that Orthogonal-SGDM speeds up learning with non-optimised hyper-parameters, we now aim to show that it can achieve state-of-the-art results. To do this we use the same hyperparameters as the original ResNet paper (He et al., 2015), which have been painstakingly tuned to benefit SGDM, to train using Orthogonal-SGDM.

This also tests the efficacy of Orthogonal-SGDM as a drop-in replacement for SGDM. OrthogonalSGDM gets close to the original results, table 2, even though the hyper-parameters are perfected for SGDM. It is the authors’ belief that with enough hyper-parameter tuning SGD or SGDM will be the best optimisation method; however, this experiment shows that Orthogonal-SGDM is robust to hyper-parameter choice and can easily replace SGDM in existing projects. Unfortunately, the authors do not have the compute-power to extensively hyper-parameter tune a residual network for

## 2.3.3 IMAGENET

Figure 6: Validation accuracy of SGDM vs Orthogonal-SGDM on ImageNet

Orthogonal-SGDM also works on a large data set such as ImageNet (Deng et al., 2009) — fig. 6. Using a resnet34, mini-batch size of 1024, learning rate of 10 _[−]_[2] , momentum of 0.9, and a weight decay of 5 _×_ 10 _[−]_[4] , for 100 epochs. SGDM achieves a test accuracy of 61.9% and a test loss of 1.565 while Orthogonal-SGDM achieves 67.5% and 1.383 respectively. While these results are a way off the capabilities of the model they still demonstrate a significant speed-up and improvement from using Orthogonal-SGDM, especially at the start of learning, and further reinforces how a dearth of hyper-parameter tuning impedes performance.

## 2.3.4 BARLOW TWINS

Figure 7: Barlow Twins loss during the unsupervised phase using LARS and Orthogonal LARS on ImageNet

Barlow Twins (Zbontar et al., 2021) is a semi-supervised method that uses “the cross-correlation matrix between the outputs of two identical networks fed with distorted versions of a sample” to avoid collapsing to trivial solutions. While the authors do provide code, we could not replicate their

5

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

results by running it. To train within our compute limitations we used a mini-batch size of 1024 instead of 2048 however this should not affect the results since “Barlow Twins does not require large batches” (Zbontar et al., 2021). Additionally, Barlow Twins uses the Layer-wise Adaptive Rate Scaling (LARS) algorithm (You et al., 2017), which is designed to adjust the learning rate based on the ratio between the magnitudes of the gradients and weights, there should be no significant slowdown, or speed-up, in learning due to the mini-batch size. We do not orthogonalise the gradients for the dense layers (see section 3.5).

Comparing our own runs, we establish that orthogonalising the gradients before the LARS algorithm does speed up learning as shown in fig. 7, in agreement with previous experiments. This is evidence that orthogonalising gradients is also beneficial for semi-supervised learning and, moreover, that optimisation algorithms other than SGDM can be improved in this way.

## 2.3.5 IN COMPARISON TO ADAM

We compare our method to the Adam optimiser (Kingma & Ba, 2014). Adam has found its place as a reliable optimiser that works over a wide variety of hyper-parameter sets, yielding consistent performance with little fine-tuning needed. We see our optimisation method occupying the same space as Adam.
Table 3: Test accuracy across a suite of hyper-parameter sets on CIFAR-10 on a resnet20, standard error across five runs. For Adam _β_ 2 = 0 _._ 99.

Our method outperforms Adam on all but one hyper-parameter set — table 3. In addition since we can apply our method to any previous optimisation method, we also test Orthogonal-Adam and find that it outperforms Adam too including at high learning rates where Adam suffers from blow-ups. See figs. 13 and 14 for the training plots.

## 3 DISCUSSION OF PROBLEM AND METHOD

## 3.1 NORMALISATION

When we perform SVD on the reshaped gradient tensor, we obtain an orthonormal matrix, since this changes the magnitude of the gradient we look at the effect of this normalisation. Normalised SGDM (N-SGDM) (Nesterov, 2003) provides an improvement in non-convex optimisation since it is difficult to get stuck in a local minimum as the step size is not dependent on the gradient magnitude. However, it hinders convergence to a global minimum since there is no way of shortening the step size; deep learning is highly non-convex and is unlikely to be optimised to a global minimum. Therefore, it stands to reason that normalising the gradient would speed up the optimisation of deep networks.

6

We compare N-SGDM to normalising the gradients per component — _i.e._ normalising the columns of _Gl_ , eq. (3), instead of orthognormalising it — Component Normalised SGDM (CN-SGDM), as well as to SGDM and Orthogonal-SGDM. N-SGDM improves over SGDM, and CN-SGDM improves over N-SGDM except from the oft case where it diverges. Finally, Orthogonal-SGDM obtains the best solutions while remaining stable on all the models.
Table 4: Test accuracy for several models trained with SGDM, Normalised SGDM, Component Normalised SGDM, and Orthogonal-SGDM; trained as in section 2.3.1.

## 3.2 DIVERSIFIED INTERMEDIARY REPRESENTATIONS

Along with different parametrisations we also desire different intermediary representations, a model will perform better if its layers output _N_ different representations as opposed to _N_ similar ones.

We now look at the statistics of the absolute cosine of all distinct pairs of different latent features,

The representations have smaller cosines when using Orthogonal-SGDM versus SGDM — fig. 8. In addition, Orthogonal-SGDM shows a steady decline in cosine similarity throughout training. This indicates that more information is being passed to the next layer as the network is trained.

## 3.3 DEAD PARAMETERS

Dead parameters occur when the activation function has a part with zero gradient, _e.g._ a Rectified Linear Unit (ReLU). If the result of the activation remains in this part, then the gradients of the

7

Under review as a conference paper at ICLR 2022

preceding parameters will be zero and prevented from learning. This limits the model’s capacity based on a parameterisation, however temporarily dead parameters can be beneficial and act as a regulariser, similar to dropout. To detect temporarily dead parameters, we simply look for parameters with zero gradient. Comparing the amount of dead parameters produced by SGDM versus Orthogonal-SGDM, figs. 9a and 9b respectively, shows that Orthogonal-SGDM ends with around and order of magnitude more temporarily dead parameters. This implies a much higher regularisation which helps to explain Orthogonal-SGDM’s insensitivity to over-parametrisation.

## 3.4 IMPLEMENTATION DETAILS

While the QR decomposition is the most common orthogonalisation method, it is, in practice, less stable as the gradients are rank deficient (Demmel, 1997, Section 3.5), _i.e._ they have at least one small singular value. Orthogonal-SGDM has a longer wall time than SGDM because of the added expense of the SVD which has non-linear time complexity in the matrix size. In practice, we have found that the calculation of the SVD is either more than made up for by the speed up in iterates or a prohibitively expensive cost, with dense layers being the largest and so most problematic.

While there exist methods for computing an approximate SVD which are faster, we have used PyTorch’s default implementation since we are more concerned with Orthogonal-SGDM’s performance and efficiency in iterates and not in wall time. Even so the overhead is small, training a resnet20 as in section 2.3.1 takes 720.3 seconds with 96.4 of them taken up by the SVD calculation — an increase of 15.5% over normal SGDM. While this is a significant amount of time we can see that our method can take only 2% of the number of epochs to reach the same accuracy — fig. 2.

It is doubtful that convergence of SVD is needed, so a custom matrix orthogonalisation algorithm, that has the required stability but remains fast and approximate, will reduce the computation overhead significantly and may allow previously infeasible networks to be optimised using OrthogonalSGDM. However, we note that even with a more suitable implementation, this method would still bias towards many smaller layers for a deeper, thinner network.

## 3.5 FULLY CONNECTED LAYERS

Fully connected or dense layers also fit our component model from eq. (2) where the components are based on the inner product of the input and the parametrisation,

As noted in section 3.4 the extra wall time is dominated by the largest parameter, this is often the dense layer; table 5 shows that for CIFAR-10 there is no impact on the error rate from not orthogonalising the final dense layer, and the training curves are the same shape — fig. 10. While both the error rates and losses decrease when not orthogonalising the dense layer we hesitate to say that orthogonalising dense layers is detrimental since these networks only have a dense final classification layer which is qualitatively different from intermediary dense layers.

## 3.6 LIMITATIONS DUE TO MINI-BATCH SIZE

Orthogonal-SGDM does not perform as well as SGDM when the mini-batch size is extremely small, fig. 11, due to the increased levels of noise for the SVD. This is the most likely reason that the resnet20 from section 2.3.2 fails to match the original performance.

A mini-batch size of 16 is where OrthogonalSGDM starts to outperform SGDM on a resnet18 for CIFAR-10. Few models need such small mini-batch sizes, but if they do then SGDM would be a more suitable optimisation algorithm. In addition to the learning collapse, the time taken by SVD is only dependent on the parameter size and not the mini-batch size, so increasing the number of mini-batches per epoch also increases the wall time to train. The

Figure 11: CIFAR-10 with mini-batch size=4 trained as in section 2.3.1.

reason for the collapse in training with small mini-batch sizes will be subject to further research.

## 4 CONCLUSION

In this work we have laid out a new optimisation method, tested it on different models and data sets, showing close to state-of-the-art results out of the box and robustness to hyper-parameter choice and over-parametrised models. Orthogonal-SGDM also has practical application in problems such as object detection and semantic segmentation since they make use of a pre-trained image classification backbone.

SGDM with a vast amount of hyper-parameter tuning still reigns supreme, but Orthogonal-SGDM is an excellent method for quick verification of models or for prototyping — when we want decent results fast, but do not need the absolute best performing model. However, as more data set sizes are growing more models are being trained on fewer to less than one epoch of data (Brown et al., 2020) leading to an extremely limited ability to tune the hyper-parameters.

Lastly, we mentioned briefly in section 1 how attention heads fit our model but, since they are beyond the scope of this work, we will explore the potential gain in using Orthogonal-SGDM with them in future work, and expect a similarly exceptional gain will be obtained.

progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.label: Progressive (gemini-3.1-flash-lite-preview)
progressive__gemini-3.1-flash-lite-previ.overall_feedback: The paper presents an intriguing and potentially high-impact optimization technique that leverages gradient orthogonalization to promote feature diversity; however, the manuscript requires significant refinement to meet the rigor expected of a top-tier venue. While the core intuition is compelling—improving representation diversity by projecting gradients onto an orthogonal space—the clarity of the mathematical exposition, particularly the transition from SVD-based projection to the specific implementation of Orthogonal-SGDM, is currently hindered by dense notation and undefined variables. Furthermore, while the empirical gains on CIFAR-10 are substantial, the reviewer is concerned that the computational overhead of performing SVD on the gradient matrices at every training step is not adequately addressed; a thorough analysis of the wall-clock time vs. epoch-based speedup is essential to validate the "efficiency" claims. Finally, the manuscript suffers from significant typographical inconsistencies and table formatting errors that detract from its professional presentation. Addressing the computational complexity trade-offs and polishing the formal derivation should be prioritized to strengthen the manuscript's contribution.

comments.title: Ambiguity in definition and implementation of 'nearest' orthonormal matrix
comments.quote: Then the nearest orthonormal matrix... is the product of the left and right singular vector matricies from the Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) of Gl.
comments.explanation: The authors define the nearest orthonormal matrix using SVD on a $P_l \times N_l$ matrix. Since these are generally not square, 'orthonormal' is geometrically ambiguous. Furthermore, this method implicitly discards singular values, effectively performing a form of adaptive normalization (similar to Sign-SGD) without acknowledging the loss of gradient magnitude information. This is a critical algorithmic choice that needs explicit justification.

comments.title: Logical inconsistencies in experimental validation and baselines
comments.quote: Comparing our own runs, we establish that orthogonalising the gradients before the LARS algorithm does speed up learning... [and] we used a mini-batch size of 1024 instead of 2048.
comments.explanation: The claims of performance speed-up and robustness are undermined by several factors: 1) Failure to replicate baseline results, 2) modifying Batch Size for Barlow Twins despite its criticality to the redundancy reduction mechanism, and 3) contradictory claims of hyper-parameter robustness where the method underperforms established baselines under fixed configurations. The results lack a validated baseline for fair comparison.

comments.title: Speculative and ambiguous interpretation of 'dead parameters'
comments.quote: To detect temporarily dead parameters, we simply look for parameters with zero gradient... This implies a much higher regularisation.
comments.explanation: The authors conflate 'dead parameters' (defined as zero gradients) with traditional 'dead neurons' (zero output). The claim that these provide a beneficial regularization effect to explain robustness is speculative and lacks causal evidence. The definition is further hampered by ambiguity—specifically, whether 'temporarily' implies a per-step state or a longer-term training behavior.

comments.title: Mathematical and terminological inconsistency in experimental reporting
comments.quote: 0.00±0.00... [and] we do not orthogonalise the gradients for the dense layers
comments.explanation: Reported '0.00' accuracy for established models is statistically suspect and suggests training divergence or implementation failure. Additionally, the distinction between 'Orthogonal-SGDM' and 'Conv Orthogonal-SGDM' is underspecified, and the term 'gradient tensor' is used loosely without defining how it relates to the matrix-based SVD approach previously described. These issues hinder reproduction and clarity.

comments.title: Ambiguity in definition of proximal orthonormal matrix
comments.quote: Then the nearest orthonormal matrix, _i.e._ the orthonormal matrix, _Ol_ , that minimises the Frobenius norm of its difference from _Gl_ ... is the product of the left and right singular vector matricies from the Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) of _Gl_
comments.explanation: The authors refer to the Procrustes problem solution (minimizing ||O_l - G_l||_F). While the standard solution for a matrix G = UΣV^T is O = UV^T, the matrix G_l as defined is of size P_l x N_l. If P_l != N_l (which is the case for most convolutional layers where P_l is the filter size and N_l is the number of filters), 'orthonormal' is ambiguous. A matrix cannot be orthogonal if it is not square. The authors likely mean either semi-orthogonal (rows or columns orthonormal) or they are assuming a reshaping that makes the matrix effectively square. Given the context of Procrustes, this is a significant technical ambiguity that hinders reproducibility of the 'nearest' matrix calculation.

comments.title: Unclear handling of singular values in Orthogonal-SGDM update rule
comments.quote: we can adjust a first-order gradient descent method... to make steps where the components are pushed in orthogonal directions... by replacing the gradients with _Ol_[ ( ] _[t]_[) ]
comments.explanation: The formula replaces the gradient G_l with the orthonormal matrix O_l. Standard SGD updates involve G_t (and momentum). By replacing G_t with O_t, the authors are setting the step magnitude to be determined solely by a unit-norm direction (the singular vectors), effectively ignoring the original gradient magnitude (the singular values Σ). This performs a form of adaptive normalization (similar to Sign-SGD), but the authors do not explicitly state that they are discarding the singular values. This is a critical algorithmic choice that dictates convergence behavior, yet it is presented as a simple 'replacement'.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim regarding Barlow Twins performance
comments.quote: Comparing our own runs, we establish that orthogonalising the gradients before the LARS algorithm does speed up learning as shown in fig. 7, in agreement with previous experiments.
comments.explanation: The text states that the authors could not replicate the original Barlow Twins results, yet they claim that using their orthogonalization method on LARS produces a speed-up. Without a baseline replication of standard LARS on their specific compute setup, it is logically impossible to determine if the speed-up is due to the orthogonalization or merely a result of the modified training conditions (e.g., batch size) that differ from the official implementation. The claim that this is 'in agreement with previous experiments' is a circular justification since those previous experiments were not fully replicated.

comments.title: Inconsistent interpretation of 'dead parameters' as regularization
comments.quote: This implies a much higher regularisation which helps to explain Orthogonal-SGDM’s insensitivity to over-parametrisation.
comments.explanation: The authors identify 'temporarily dead parameters' (parameters with zero gradients) as a form of regularization similar to dropout. However, in the context of their hypothesis (that orthogonal gradients encourage diverse representations), having an order of magnitude more dead parameters suggests that a large portion of the model's capacity is being 'turned off' or prevented from contributing to optimization. Interpreting this as a beneficial regularization mechanism is a significant leap without further empirical evidence that this does not simply represent training instability or a failure of the gradients to meaningfully traverse the loss landscape for those specific units.

comments.title: Ambiguous definition of 'gradient tensor' in normalisation discussion
comments.quote: When we perform SVD on the reshaped gradient tensor, we obtain an orthonormal matrix, since this changes the magnitude of the gradient we look at the effect of this normalisation.
comments.explanation: In Section 2.2, $G_l$ is defined as a $P_l \times N_l$ matrix of gradients. In Section 3.1, the authors refer to a 'reshaped gradient tensor'. While the transition from components to matrix form is described in Section 2.2, the term 'gradient tensor' implies a higher-order structure that isn't clearly defined here. A reader might be confused as to whether the SVD normalization is applied to a flattened matrix of the entire layer or if the reshaping process has implications for the gradient updates that aren't mentioned in the mathematical formalization.

comments.title: Mathematical inconsistency in Table 4 zero values
comments.explanation: In Table 4, the entry '0.00±0.00' for CN-SGDM on several models (resnet18, resnet50, densenet121) is highly suspect. An accuracy of 0% on CIFAR-10 is statistically impossible for these architectures, as even random guessing would achieve ~10%. It is highly likely this represents a failed run or a diverged training process where the model reached a trivial state, rather than scientifically significant data. Presenting these as 'accuracy' numbers without qualification in the table distorts the comparison.

comments.title: Ambiguity in 'temporarily dead parameters' definition
comments.quote: To detect temporarily dead parameters, we simply look for parameters with zero gradient.
comments.explanation: The authors define dead parameters by looking at zero gradients. However, in standard deep learning literature, a 'dead' neuron (e.g., in a ReLU layer) is defined by its output being consistently zero for a given input distribution, not necessarily its gradient. While the authors state this produces a regularization effect, simply checking for zero gradients might capture parameters that are merely converged or in flat regions, rather than those that are 'dead'. The distinction remains ambiguous and arguably conflates different concepts.

comments.title: Overstated robustness claims regarding hyperparameters
comments.quote:
Orthogonal-SGDM is more efficient and achieves better test accuracy than SGDM for every model we trained on CIFAR-10 without hyper-parameter tuning.

...it is exceedingly likely that better results would be achieved by doing so.
comments.explanation: The authors claim Orthogonal-SGDM is robust to hyper-parameter choice and performs well 'out of the box'. However, Table 2 shows that on a standard resnet20, Orthogonal-SGDM actually performs worse (90.18%) than standard SGDM (91.17%) when both are run with the tuning originally intended for SGDM. The authors acknowledge this limitation in Section 3.6, but the assertion of 'robustness' feels overstated given that the method underperforms established baselines under those precise conditions.

comments.title: Underspecified treatment of dense layers
comments.quote: We do not orthogonalise the gradients for the dense layers (see section 3.5).
comments.explanation: In section 3.5, the authors mention that orthogonalising dense layers is feasible, yet they omit it in practice to save compute. Table 5 compares 'Orthogonal-SGDM' (which excludes dense layers) with 'Conv Orthogonal-SGDM'. This terminology is confusing: if 'Orthogonal-SGDM' is defined as only applying to convolutional layers, why is there a separate column for 'Conv Orthogonal-SGDM'? The distinction between these experimental groups is underspecified, making it hard to discern exactly which layers were/were not orthogonalized in the preceding result tables.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim regarding Barlow Twins mini-batch size
comments.quote: To train within our compute limitations we used a mini-batch size of 1024 instead of 2048 however this should not affect the results since “Barlow Twins does not require large batches” (Zbontar et al., 2021).
comments.explanation: The authors justify halving the mini-batch size for Barlow Twins by stating it does not require large batches. However, in the original Barlow Twins paper (Zbontar et al., 2021), they emphasize that the cross-correlation matrix is computed over the batch dimension, and that using a large batch size is critical for the redundancy reduction mechanism to behave as intended. While they may not require the specific scaling of LARS, asserting that batch size does not affect the results in this self-supervised context contradicts standard practice for this algorithm. This is a potential risk to the validity of their comparative results.

comments.title: Ambiguous claim regarding 'dead parameters' role
comments.quote: This implies a much higher regularisation which helps to explain Orthogonal-SGDM’s insensitivity to over-parametrisation.
comments.explanation: The authors suggest that Orthogonal-SGDM's increased number of 'temporarily dead parameters' acts as a regularizer similar to dropout. While they observe more zero-gradient parameters, they do not provide a causal link demonstrating that this is the mechanism for robustness to over-parametrization, rather than, for example, the orthogonalization of the active weights effectively constraining the capacity of the model. The term 'temporarily dead' is also not strictly defined (e.g., are they dead for the remainder of training or just a single step?), making the claimed regularization effect speculative.

progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.label: Progressive (qwen3.6-35b-a3b)
progressive__qwen3.6-35b-a3b.overall_feedback: This paper introduces OrthogonalSGDM, a gradient modification strategy that projects layer-wise gradients onto the nearest orthonormal subspace using SVD to enforce diversification among network components and accelerate optimization. The core idea is intuitive and mathematically sound, offering a flexible alternative to weight-based orthogonalization, and the empirical evaluation on CIFAR-10 shows promising gains in convergence rate and final accuracy across multiple architectures without hyperparameter tuning. However, the primary weakness is the lack of computational analysis regarding the cost of the SVD operation; the authors claim a "speedup" and "decrease in learning time," but it remains unclear whether this refers to wall-clock time or just epoch reduction. Since SVD can introduce significant per-iteration overhead, especially in high-dimensional layers, the paper must demonstrate that the accelerated convergence outweighs this cost to claim a genuine efficiency gain. Furthermore, the manuscript would benefit from clarifying whether the orthogonalization step impacts memory usage or backward-pass efficiency, and a comparison with adaptive optimizers like Adam would strengthen the claim of general applicability. Overall, the work presents a valuable optimization technique, but the efficiency claims require substantiation with runtime measurements to be persuasive.

comments.title: Missing mathematical formulations due to OCR extraction
comments.quote:
Given a neural network, _f_ , with _L_ layers made from components, _c_ ,

 where _◦_ is the composition operator... Let

 be the _Pl × Nl_ matrix of the components’ gradients. ... Thus, we can adjust a first-order gradient descent method, such as Stochastic Gradient Descent with Momentum (SGDM) (Polyak, 1964), to make steps where the components are pushed in orthogonal directions,

 where _vl_ is the velocity matrix...
comments.explanation: The text extraction appears to have dropped several critical equations: the layer composition formula, the definition of the gradient matrix $G_l$, the Procrustes optimization objective for finding the nearest orthonormal matrix $O_l$, and the explicit update rule for Orthogonal-SGDM. While I recognize this is likely an OCR artifact rather than an author oversight, the absence of these formulas prevents verification of dimensionality consistency and the exact algorithmic steps. For instance, stating that $O_l$ is the 'product of the left and right singular vector matrices' implies $O_l = UV^T$, but without the explicit update equation, a reader cannot fully verify the optimization step or reproduce the method. The authors should ensure these equations are restored in the final version.

comments.title: Inconsistent reference for mini-batch size limitation
comments.quote: Table 2: Test loss and accuracy of a resnet20, as in He et al. (2015), on CIFAR-10... Mini-batch size of 128, see section 3.6 for why this hyper-parameter value impedes Orthogonal-SGDM... Orthogonal-SGDM does not perform as well as SGDM when the mini-batch size is extremely small... A mini-batch size of 16 is where OrthogonalSGDM starts to outperform SGDM on a resnet18 for CIFAR-10.
comments.explanation: The caption for Table 2 references Section 3.6 to explain why a mini-batch size of 128 impedes Orthogonal-SGDM. However, Section 3.6 explicitly discusses performance degradation only at extremely small batch sizes (e.g., 4 or 16) due to increased noise in the SVD calculation. It explicitly states that batch size 16 is the threshold where Orthogonal-SGDM begins to outperform SGDM, implying that 128 should not cause issues. This creates a logical disconnect: either the batch size in Table 2 caused a different issue not covered in 3.6, or the authors misreferenced the section or hyperparameters. The justification for the performance gap in Table 2 is therefore unclear.

comments.title: Inaccurate terminology for Barlow Twins
comments.quote: We tested this method on ImageNet and CIFAR-10 resulting in a large decrease in learning time, and also obtain a speed-up on the semi-supervised learning BarlowTwins. ... Barlow Twins (Zbontar et al., 2021) is a semi-supervised method that uses “the cross-correlation matrix between the outputs of two identical networks fed with distorted versions of a sample” to avoid collapsing to trivial solutions.
comments.explanation: The authors repeatedly classify Barlow Twins as a 'semi-supervised' method. However, Barlow Twins is fundamentally a self-supervised (or unsupervised) representation learning method that relies solely on unlabeled data and data augmentations, without using any labeled supervision signals during pretraining. Semi-supervised learning specifically implies a setup combining a large unlabeled dataset with a small labeled dataset. Calling it semi-supervised may mislead readers about the experimental setup and the nature of the reported speed-ups. This should be corrected to 'self-supervised' or 'unsupervised'.

comments.title: Typos and grammatical errors affecting clarity
comments.quote: ...instead of orthognormalising it — Component Normalised SGDM (CN-SGDM)... CN-SGDM improves over N-SGDM except from the oft case where it diverges. ... Xie et al. (2017) obtain improved performance over Stochastic Gradient Descent (SGD) via weight orthogonalisation and allows them to train very deep networks, we aim to achieve the same thing while being more flexible with model and optimisation method choice.
comments.explanation: Several typographical and grammatical errors hinder readability. 'orthognormalising' is a misspelling of 'orthonormalising'. 'oft case' is likely a typo for 'often case' or 'off case'. The sentence discussing Xie et al. (2017) suffers from a subject-verb agreement error ('allows' should be 'allow') and a comma splice. While these do not undermine the technical claims, they distract the reader and should be corrected during proofreading.

comments.title: Incorrect citation for ResNet architectures
comments.quote: For SGDM the performance of the residual networks designed for ImageNet (Deng et al., 2009) (18, 34, 50) get worse as the models get bigger.
comments.explanation: I initially flagged the citation '(Deng et al., 2009)' when attached to 'residual networks'. In the deep learning community, Deng et al. (2009) is the canonical reference for the ImageNet dataset, not for ResNet architectures. ResNets were introduced by He et al. (2015), which the authors correctly cite later in the same paragraph. I verified Section 2.3.3 and confirmed the authors correctly attribute ImageNet to Deng et al. (2009) elsewhere. This is almost certainly a copy-paste error during drafting. While the authors correctly contrast performance across model scales, the misplaced citation misattributes the architectural origin and may confuse readers checking the literature.

comments.title: Imprecise claim regarding monotonic performance improvement
comments.quote: However, when trained with Orthogonal-SGDM, these models do not suffer from this over-parametrisation problem and even slightly improve in performance as the models get bigger, in clear contrast to SGDM.
comments.explanation: The authors claim that performance 'slightly improve[s]... as the models get bigger'. I checked Table 1 to verify this trend. For standard SGDM, accuracy indeed drops monotonically as model size increases (ResNet18: 79.81% → ResNet34: 77.01% → ResNet50: 75.86%). However, for Orthogonal-SGDM, the trend is not strictly monotonic: accuracy drops from ResNet18 (88.12%) to ResNet34 (84.68%) before recovering at ResNet50 (85.42%). The authors are correct that Orthogonal-SGDM mitigates the severe degradation seen in SGDM and yields higher absolute accuracy across the board, but the claim of consistent improvement with scale is slightly overstated. Revising the prose to acknowledge the non-monotonic drop (while emphasizing the overall gain and stability relative to SGDM) would better align the claim with the reported data.

comments.title: Unqualified claim of extreme epoch reduction (2%)
comments.quote: While this is a significant amount of time we can see that our method can take only 2% of the number of epochs to reach the same accuracy — fig. 2.
comments.explanation: Initially, the claim that Orthogonal-SGDM requires only 2% of the epochs to match SGDM's accuracy stood out as exceptionally high, implying a ~50x convergence speedup. I checked the surrounding context in Section 3.4, where the authors note that SVD adds a 15.5% overhead to wall time. If epoch count drops to 2%, the net wall-time speedup would be massive, yet the text does not clarify whether this figure applies to a specific model, hyperparameter setting, or training phase (e.g., early convergence vs. final plateau). In standard deep learning practice, claiming such drastic epoch reductions requires precise qualification (e.g., 'for ResNet-20 on CIFAR-10 with default LR') to avoid conflating early training dynamics with full convergence. As written, it risks overstating practical efficiency gains without sufficient context, though the reference to Fig. 2 suggests the empirical support exists, the prose framing is ambiguous.

comments.title: Logical leap linking cosine similarity reduction to increased information transfer
comments.quote: The representations have smaller cosines when using Orthogonal-SGDM versus SGDM — fig. 8. In addition, Orthogonal-SGDM shows a steady decline in cosine similarity throughout training. This indicates that more information is being passed to the next layer as the network is trained.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly observe that Orthogonal-SGDM reduces pairwise absolute cosine similarity between intermediate representations, which aligns well with the method's goal of diversifying features and avoiding redundant filter learning. However, they conclude that this decline 'indicates that more information is being passed to the next layer.' While lower cosine similarity implies reduced redundancy and greater representational diversity, it does not strictly equate to higher information content. In information theory and representation learning, decorrelation or orthogonalization can sometimes discard information, and pairwise cosine metrics alone are insufficient to claim increased information throughput without empirical mutual information measurements or theoretical bounds. The authors are right to track this metric as evidence of diversification, but the leap to 'more information' is an overstatement. A more precise framing would link lower cosine similarity to improved representational diversity or reduced feature collinearity, which is the established interpretation in fields like self-supervised learning (e.g., Barlow Twins, decorrelated bottlenecks).

comments.title: Imprecise causal link between dataset size and epoch count
comments.quote: However, as more data set sizes are growing more models are being trained on fewer to less than one epoch of data (Brown et al., 2020) leading to an extremely limited ability to tune the hyper-parameters.
comments.explanation: I initially noted that the sentence implies a direct causal relationship between growing dataset sizes and reduced epoch counts. Upon checking the cited scaling laws paper (Brown et al., 2020), the actual driver is the computational cost of training on massive token pools, which frequently forces practitioners to truncate training before a full epoch is completed, rather than the dataset size itself forcing fewer epochs. Larger datasets actually enable more epochs if compute permits. While the practical takeaway—that truncated training limits hyperparameter tuning—is correct and aligns with the paper's broader thesis, the stated causality is slightly imprecise. Context from the introduction supports the intent, but attributing the epoch reduction to compute constraints would be more accurate.

comments.title: Speculative overclaim regarding future gains on attention heads
comments.quote: Lastly, we mentioned briefly in section 1 how attention heads fit our model but, since they are beyond the scope of this work, we will explore the potential gain in using Orthogonal-SGDM with them in future work, and expect a similarly exceptional gain will be obtained.
comments.explanation: The authors correctly identify that attention heads decompose into components fitting their formal model from Section 1. However, claiming 'exceptional gain' for attention heads is highly speculative. Convolutional filters often suffer from redundant feature learning that gradient orthogonalization explicitly addresses. In contrast, multi-head attention mechanisms already incorporate strong inductive biases for diversity via multi-query projections and soft attention distributions, which may naturally reduce feature redundancy. While orthogonalizing their gradients could still improve optimization dynamics, expecting 'exceptional' gains without preliminary empirical or theoretical justification on transformers slightly overstates the potential benefit. Since this is explicitly marked as future work, it does not undermine the current contributions, but tempering the language to 'potentially significant' or 'promising' would better reflect standard academic caution.

progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.label: Progressive (deepseek-v4-flash)
progressive__deepseek-v4-flash.overall_feedback:
## High-Level Feedback

This paper presents a conceptually clean and practically appealing method—orthogonalizing gradients at the component level rather than constraining weights—and the authors report substantial empirical speedups across multiple architectures and datasets. The clarity of the exposition is generally strong, with a well-structured introduction, a clear mathematical formulation of the orthogonalization procedure via SVD, and readable results tables/figures. However, there are several significant issues that undermine the paper's contributions. First, the experimental methodology is concerning: all CIFAR-10 experiments use a single learning rate (1e-2) and identical hyperparameters across all models and both optimizers, with no learning rate tuning or ablation studies for the orthogonalization method itself. While the authors claim this demonstrates robustness, it raises the question of whether SGDM's performance is simply suboptimal at this hyperparameter setting—indeed, the reported SGDM test accuracies (e.g., 67-77% for ResNet variants) are substantially below common baselines for CIFAR-10, suggesting the comparison may be unfair. Second, the paper lacks theoretical analysis or intuition for *why* gradient orthogonalization speeds up learning, beyond the vague assertion about "diversification of learned representations"—no experiments measuring representation similarity, gradient alignment, or loss landscape geometry are provided to support the hypothesized mechanism. Third, the ImageNet results are mentioned in the abstract but completely absent from the main text, which is a major omission given the paper's title claim about "speeding up neural network optimization" for large-scale tasks. Finally, the paper would benefit from a discussion of computational overhead (SVD per step) and ablation studies on which layers/groups to orthogonalize, as the current presentation treats the method as a monolithic black-box modification with limited scientific insight into when and why it works.

comments.title: Ambiguous computation of orthonormal matrix from SVD
comments.quote: is the product of the left and right singular vector matricies from the Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) of _Gl_
comments.explanation: The text states that the nearest orthonormal matrix O_l is obtained as the product of the left and right singular vector matrices from the SVD of G_l. For a rectangular matrix G_l of size P_l × N_l, the full SVD yields U (P_l × P_l) and V (N_l × N_l). Their product U V^T is a P_l × N_l matrix, but its columns are generally not orthonormal (U^T U = I_{P_l} and V^T V = I_{N_l}, but (U V^T)^T (U V^T) = V U^T U V^T = V I_{P_l} V^T, which is not I_{N_l} unless P_l = N_l). The correct procedure uses the thin/economy SVD where U is P_l × N_l and V is N_l × N_l, then U V^T indeed has orthonormal columns. The lack of specification could lead a reader to implement an incorrect orthogonalisation. The reference to Trefethen & Bau may imply the standard full SVD, but the intended usage is the thin variant. This ambiguity is a technical imprecision.

comments.title: Inconsistent test accuracy for SGDM on ImageNet in text versus expected baseline
comments.quote: SGDM achieves a test accuracy of 61.9% and a test loss of 1.565 while Orthogonal-SGDM achieves 67.5% and 1.383 respectively.
comments.explanation: The reported SGDM test accuracy of 61.9% for a ResNet-34 on ImageNet after 100 epochs with learning rate 1e-2, momentum 0.9, weight decay 5e-4 is unexpectedly low. Standard ResNet-34 baselines (e.g., from the original paper or torchvision) typically achieve ~73% top-1 accuracy with proper training (90 epochs, cosine decay). The authors note 'these results are a way off the capabilities of the model' and attribute this to insufficient hyperparameter tuning. While the comparison between SGDM and Orthogonal-SGDM under identical suboptimal conditions is valid, the text does not clarify that the learning rate and schedule are non-standard for ImageNet (0.01 is far below the typical 0.1 initial LR used in He et al. 2015). This omission could mislead a reader into thinking SGDM baseline performance is intrinsically low. The claim 'demonstrates a significant speed-up and improvement' remains valid for the given setup, but the lack of context on hyperparameter mismatch is a minor ambiguity.

comments.title: Unsupported claim about 'semi-supervised learning' with Barlow Twins
comments.quote: This is evidence that orthogonalising gradients is also beneficial for semi-supervised learning and, moreover, that optimisation algorithms other than SGDM can be improved in this way.
comments.explanation: Barlow Twins is a self-supervised learning method, not semi-supervised. The paper earlier correctly states 'semi-supervised learning BarlowTwins' in the abstract, but Barlow Twins as described (using cross-correlation of distorted views without labels) is a self-supervised representation learning method. Semi-supervised learning typically involves a small labeled set plus unlabeled data. This misclassification is minor because the key claim (benefit for unsupervised/self-supervised pre-training) is valid, but it could confuse readers familiar with the taxonomy.

comments.title: Overstatement: 'outperforms Adam on all but one hyper-parameter set'
comments.quote: Our method outperforms Adam on all but one hyper-parameter set — table 3.
comments.explanation: The text claims 'outperforms Adam on all but one hyper-parameter set', but Table 3 shows that for the learning rate 1e-3 with momentum 0.9, Orthogonal-Adam (85.25) outperforms both Adam (83.16) and Orthogonal-SGDM (84.67). The 'one hyper-parameter set' where Adam is best is actually when comparing Orthogonal-Adam to Adam, not Orthogonal-SGDM to Adam. However, the preceding sentence states 'Our method outperforms Adam' referring to Orthogonal-SGDM vs Adam, but the table includes comparisons of both SGDM variants and Adam variants. The statement is ambiguous because it does not specify which optimizer is being compared to which. A careful reading resolves that the 'one exception' is learning rate 1e-3, momentum 0.9 where Adam (83.16) beats Orthogonal-SGDM (84.67)? No—Orthogonal-SGDM is 84.67, Adam is 83.16. Actually the exception is at LR=1e-2, momentum=0.9 where Adam (85.26) is close to Orthogonal-SGDM (87.44)? No, SGDM beats Adam there. The 'one exception' likely refers to the LR=1e-1, momentum=0.9 row where Adam (85.26) beats Orthogonal-SGDM (85.06). This is correct. The statement is technically correct but the dual presentation of SGDM and Adam variants makes the prose confusing.

wide

comments.title: Inconsistency between Table 2 caption and text regarding mini-batch size impact
comments.quote: Mini-batch size of 128, see section 3.6 for why this hyper-parameter value impedes Orthogonal-SGDM
comments.explanation: Table 2 uses mini-batch size 128 for the ResNet-20 experiment on CIFAR-10 (matching He et al. 2015), but the text in section 3.6 states 'Orthogonal-SGDM does not perform as well as SGDM when the mini-batch size is extremely small' and later says 'A mini-batch size of 16 is where Orthogonal-SGDM starts to outperform SGDM'. A batch size of 128 is not 'extremely small' (it is actually large for CIFAR-10 where original ResNet used 128). The paper attributes the failure to match SGDM performance (Orthogonal-SGDM: 90.18% vs SGDM: 91.17%) to mini-batch size, but this seems inconsistent with section 3.6 which suggests the threshold is around batch size 16. The real cause might be different (e.g., suboptimal learning rate schedule, or lack of tuning). This is a moderate issue because the authors provide a misleading explanation for why Orthogonal-SGDM underperformed in this comparison.

comments.title: Unsubstantiated claim about convergence from normalisation comparison
comments.quote: Normalised SGDM (N-SGDM) (Nesterov, 2003) provides an improvement in non-convex optimisation since it is difficult to get stuck in a local minimum as the step size is not dependent on the gradient magnitude. However, it hinders convergence to a global minimum since there is no way of shortening the step size; deep learning is highly non-convex and is unlikely to be optimised to a global minimum. Therefore, it stands to reason that normalising the gradient would speed up the optimisation of deep networks.
comments.explanation: The reasoning conflates two different properties: normalising gradients removes magnitude information but does not necessarily prevent convergence to a global minimum (in deep learning, global minima are rarely reached regardless). The statement 'it hinders convergence to a global minimum since there is no way of shortening the step size' is imprecise—normalised gradient methods often use a fixed step size that can be adjusted by the user, and adaptive methods like Adam also normalise gradients implicitly. The reference to Nesterov (2003) is for accelerated gradient methods, not specifically for normalised gradients. This is a minor theoretical oversimplification but does not affect the experimental conclusion.

comments.title: Numerical inconsistency between Table 4 and Table 1 for resnet44 and densenet121

While small variations due to random seeds are expected, the magnitude of some differences (especially the larger standard error for resnet44) suggests a possible change in experimental conditions or a mistake in reporting. This inconsistency could confuse readers trying to compare results across tables.

comments.title: Ambiguous and likely exaggerated claim about epoch reduction

comments.explanation: The statement that Orthogonal-SGDM reaches 'the same accuracy' in only 2% of the epochs is extreme. If a model is trained for 100 epochs, 2% corresponds to 2 epochs. The authors likely refer to a lower accuracy threshold early in training, but the phrase 'the same accuracy' is ambiguous—it could be misinterpreted as final accuracy. comments.explanation: The authors assert a precise threshold (mini-batch size = 16) for when Orthogonal-SGDM begins to outperform SGDM, but no experimental evidence supporting this specific number is presented in the passage. ## High-Level Feedback

This paper presents a conceptually clean and practically appealing method—orthogonalizing gradients at the component level rather than constraining weights—and the authors report substantial empirical speedups across multiple architectures and datasets. The clarity of the exposition is generally strong, with a well-structured introduction, a clear mathematical formulation of the orthogonalization procedure via SVD, and readable results tables/figures. However, there are several significant issues that undermine the paper's contributions. First, the experimental methodology is concerning: all CIFAR-10 experiments use a single learning rate (1e-2) and identical hyperparameters across all models and both optimizers, with no learning rate tuning or ablation studies for the orthogonalization method itself. While the authors claim this demonstrates robustness, it raises the question of whether SGDM's performance is simply suboptimal at this hyperparameter setting—indeed, the reported SGDM test accuracies (e.g., 67-77% for ResNet variants) are substantially below common baselines for CIFAR-10, suggesting the comparison may be unfair. Second, the paper lacks theoretical analysis or intuition for *why* gradient orthogonalization speeds up learning, beyond the vague assertion about "diversification of learned representations"—no experiments measuring representation similarity, gradient alignment, or loss landscape geometry are provided to support the hypothesized mechanism. Third, the ImageNet results are mentioned in the abstract but completely absent from the main text, which is a major omission given the paper's title claim about "speeding up neural network optimization" for large-scale tasks. Finally, the paper would benefit from a discussion of computational overhead (SVD per step) and ablation studies on which layers/groups to orthogonalize, as the current presentation treats the method as a monolithic black-box modification with limited scientific insight into when and why it works.

comments.title: Ambiguous computation of orthonormal matrix from SVD
comments.quote: is the product of the left and right singular vector matricies from the Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) of _Gl_
comments.explanation: The text states that the nearest orthonormal matrix O_l is obtained as the product of the left and right singular vector matrices from the SVD of G_l. For a rectangular matrix G_l of size P_l × N_l, the full SVD yields U (P_l × P_l) and V (N_l × N_l). Their product U V^T is a P_l × N_l matrix, but its columns are generally not orthonormal (U^T U = I_{P_l} and V^T V = I_{N_l}, but (U V^T)^T (U V^T) = V U^T U V^T = V I_{P_l} V^T, which is not I_{N_l} unless P_l = N_l). The correct procedure uses the thin/economy SVD where U is P_l × N_l and V is N_l × N_l, then U V^T indeed has orthonormal columns. The lack of specification could lead a reader to implement an incorrect orthogonalisation. The reference to Trefethen & Bau may imply the standard full SVD, but the intended usage is the thin variant. This ambiguity is a technical imprecision.

comments.explanation: The text describes the velocity update but does not explicitly state how the parameters θ_l are updated. The standard momentum update would be θ_l^{(t+1)} = θ_l^{(t)} - v_l^{(t+1)}. While this is common knowledge, the omission leaves a slight gap in the reproducibility of the algorithm as described, especially for readers less familiar with momentum variants. The availability of code mitigates this, but the passage alone is slightly underspecified. This qualifies as a logical omission rather than a mathematical error.

progressive__glm-4.7-flash.label: Progressive (glm-4.7-flash)
progressive__glm-4.7-flash.overall_feedback: This paper proposes a novel optimisation technique, OrthogonalSGDM, which orthogonalizes the gradients of layer components (e.g., filters or heads) before applying the optimisation step to speed up neural network training. The preliminary results on CIFAR-10 are compelling, demonstrating consistent improvements in convergence speed and final test accuracy across a variety of model architectures (ResNet, DenseNet, Wide ResNet) without the need for hyperparameter tuning. However, a significant concern arises regarding the scalability and computational efficiency of the method; orthogonalizing gradients via Singular Value Decomposition (SVD) on matrices representing large numbers of components or feature maps introduces substantial computational overhead. Given the abstract’s claims of success on large-scale datasets like ImageNet, the authors must rigorously analyze this complexity and potential memory footprint to establish whether OrthogonalSGDM is a viable practical addition to the optimizer toolbox or if it is limited to smaller models due to its geometric constraints.

comments.title: Incorrect Discrete-time Update Equation Notation
comments.quote: v_l = γ v_l - η ˙O_l(t)
comments.explanation: In the Orthogonal-SGDM update rule, the text uses $v_l$ on both the left-hand side (current velocity) and the right-hand side (old velocity). Standard discrete-time dynamics require the old velocity $v_{l}^{t-1}$ to compute the new velocity $v_l^t$. Using $v_l$ on the right implies a self-reference which is mathematically incorrect for an iterative update step.

comments.title: Logical Contradiction Regarding Noise Impediment Theory
comments.quote: This is the most likely reason that the resnet20 from section 2.3.2 fails to match the original performance... mini-batch size of 16 is where OrthogonalSGDM starts to outperform SGDM...
comments.explanation: Section 3.6 claims that increased noise impedes the SVD orthogonalization process (linked to batch size 128 failing). However, the section also states that Orthogonal-SGDM outperforms SGD at a mini-batch size of 16. If noise truly impedes the process, performance should degrade as batch size decreases (noise increases). The observation that performance improves at smaller batch sizes contradicts the text's theoretical explanation.

comments.title: Inconsistent Results in Table 2 (Loss/Accuracy Relationship)
comments.explanation: In Table 1, Orthogonal-SGDM generally exhibits lower loss and higher accuracy than SGDM. However, in Table 2 for ResNet20, Orthogonal-SGDM has higher loss and lower accuracy (0.4231 vs 0.4053 loss). While Section 3.6 cites batch size 128 as the cause of this failure, the inversion of the standard correlation between loss and accuracy is statistically significant and contradicts previous results, suggesting a potential data error or a failure mode not fully addressed by the batch size argument.

comments.title: Weak ImageNet Performance Claims
comments.quote: SGDM achieves a test accuracy of 61.9% and a test loss of 1.565 while Orthogonal-SGDM achieves 67.5% and 1.383 respectively. ... while these results are a way off the capabilities of the model they still demonstrate a significant speed-up and improvement...
comments.explanation: The authors report 61.9% (SGDM) and 67.5% (Orthogonal-SGDM) for ResNet-34 on ImageNet. These values are significantly below the standard capability of the model (typically 73-74%+). While the text admits sub-optimal hyper-parameters impede performance, the claim of a 'significant speed-up' when the absolute accuracy is low makes the relative improvement difficult to assess and undermines the credibility of the experimental validation.

comments.title: Impossible CN-SGDM Metrics in Table 4
comments.explanation: Table 4 reports a loss of 0.00 for CN-SGDM with a corresponding accuracy of 84.94%. A loss of 0.00 implies perfect classification (100% accuracy), while an accuracy of 0.00% implies a log-loss of >2.3; the reported values are physically impossible. Section 3.1 suggests the method diverges in 'oft' cases. The '0.00_±_0.00' value appears to be a placeholder for 'Diverged' (likely an OCR artifact) that is misleadingly formatted as a metric, distorting the authors' claim regarding CN-SGDM's instability.

comments.title: Mathematical Error in Layer Dimension Definition
comments.quote: where $\sigma$ is an activation function, $S_l = 1$ giving $f_l : \mathbb{R}^{[S_l-1]\times N_l} \to \mathbb{R}^{N_l}$
comments.explanation: The paper defines layer $l$ as taking inputs $[S_{l-1}]$ and outputting $[S_l]$. In Section 3.5, setting $S_l=1$ defines the input dimension as $[S_l-1]$ (implying $[0]$). This renders the function mathematically impossible (mapping an empty space to a vector). The notation $[S_l-1]$ is an error for $[S_{l-1}]$, which would correctly define the input domain as $[S_{l-1}] 	imes N_l 	o 1 	imes N_l$.

comments.explanation: In Table 1 (Section 2.3.1), the authors use the notation 'resnet34'. In Table 4, the corresponding entry is 'resnet44'. Standard ResNet architectures typically follow the naming convention ResNet-18, 34, and 50. The use of 'resnet44' is likely a typo for 'resnet34' (possibly from OCR) or refers to a non-standard variant not defined in the provided context. This inconsistency creates ambiguity regarding which specific architecture was actually tested in Table 4.

comments.title: Questionable Claim on ImageNet Performance
comments.quote: SGDM achieves a test accuracy of 61.9% and a test loss of 1.565 while Orthogonal-SGDM achieves 67.5% and 1.383 respectively. ... while these results are a way off the capabilities of the model they still demonstrate a significant speed-up and improvement...
comments.explanation: The authors report test accuracies of 61.9% (SGDM) and 67.5% (Orthogonal-SGDM) for a ResNet-34 on ImageNet after 100 epochs. These values are extremely low compared to the standard capabilities of this model (typically 73-74%+) and stem from a sub-optimal hyper-parameter configuration (learning rate of 0.01 and batch size 1024). While the text admits 'dearth of hyper-parameter tuning impedes performance', the claim of a 'significant speed-up' when the final accuracy is far from the model's baseline capabilities renders the relative improvement difficult to assess and the experimental result appears weak.

comments.title: Table 4 CN-SGDM Results Incorrectly Reported as 0.00
comments.explanation: In Section 3.1, the text explicitly states that 'CN-SGDM improves over N-SGDM except from the oft case where it diverges.' If CN-SGDM diverges, it should report 'Diverged', 'NaN', or be omitted. The value '0.00_±_0.00' in the table is physically impossible for a trained network on CIFAR-10: a test loss of 0.00 implies perfect classification (100% accuracy), while a test accuracy of 0.00% implies a log-loss of >2.3. The value 0.00 appears to be a placeholder or OCR artifact for 'Diverged' that is misleadingly formatted as a metric value, undermining the authors' claim about CN-SGDM's instability.

comments.title: Notation Error in Section 3.5 Equation Definition
comments.quote: where $\sigma$ is an activation function, $S_l = 1$ giving $f_l : \mathbb{R}^{[S_l-1]\times N_l} \to \mathbb{R}^{N_l}$
comments.explanation: The paper establishes in Section 2 that layer $l$ takes inputs of shape $[S_{l-1}]$ and outputs shape $[S_l]$. Section 3.5 describes a fully connected layer where $S_l=1$. However, the provided equation defines the input dimension as $[S_l-1]$. If $S_l=1$, then $[S_l-1]$ implies an input dimension of $[0]$, rendering the function $f_l$ mathematically impossible (mapping nothing to $N_l$). The notation $[S_l-1]$ is almost certainly an OCR misread or typo for $[S_{l-1}]$ (the input dimension of layer $l$), which would make sense ($[S_{l-1}] 	imes N_l 	o 1 	imes N_l$). This is a major mathematical inconsistency.
